messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) – last chance
messages.subject_clean: Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) – last chance
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 1-day seminar Generative AI for Statistical Science (Free Seminar) livestreaming March 3 with Lei Ding (Department of Statistics, University of Manitoba). This seminar introduces modern generative AI paradigms, offering powerful statistical tools for modeling complex data structures. Ecologists and environmental scientists can leverage these methods for tasks such as generating synthetic environmental datasets to explore hypothetical scenarios, imputing missing values in long-term monitoring data, or performing advanced text analysis on ecological literature and field observations. Understanding these techniques will enable researchers to enhance data analysis capabilities, address common challenges in ecological data management, and explore novel approaches to environmental modeling within their R-based workflows.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/generative-ai-for-statistical-science-fr) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
messages.subject_clean: Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse complex ecological community data with our live online
course *Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities using Vegan
(VGNR09)*.

https://prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecological-communities-using-vegan-vgnr09/

This intensive training provides a practical introduction to multivariate
statistical methods used to explore, visualise, and interpret ecological
community datasets. Using the widely used R package *vegan*, participants
will work with real data to understand patterns in species assemblages and
environmental gradients. ()

The course covers:

   -

   Exploring and visualising multivariate ecological datasets
   -

   Ordination methods such as PCA, NMDS, CA and RDA
   -

   Clustering and similarity analyses
   -

   Hypothesis testing using methods such as PERMANOVA
   -

   Interpreting multivariate outputs in ecological contexts

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 20?24 April 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?485

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals working with community or
biodiversity datasets who want practical, reproducible workflows for
analysing species assemblages in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecological-communities-using-vegan-vgnr09/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Deep Learning Using R
messages.body_plain:
Build practical deep learning skills in R with our live online course *Deep
Learning Using R (DLUR01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/

This hands-on workshop introduces modern deep learning techniques using the
R *torch* ecosystem. Participants will learn both the core theory behind
neural networks and how to implement deep learning models through guided
coding exercises suitable for real research workflows.

The course covers:

   -

   Foundations of artificial neural networks and deep learning
   -

   Building multilayer perceptrons in R
   -

   Convolutional neural networks for image data
   -

   Transformer architectures and modern deep learning workflows
   -

   Practical model training, evaluation, and interpretation in R ()

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 21?22 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?300

This course is suitable for researchers, postgraduate students, and
analysts who are comfortable with R and want a practical introduction to
neural networks, CNNs, and transformer models for scientific data analysis.

https://prstats.org/course/deep-learning-using-r-dlur01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Standard Modelling Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling
messages.subject_clean: Standard Modelling Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build robust, reproducible species distribution and ecological
niche models in our live online course *Standard Modelling Procedure for
Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling (SDMS01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/

This applied R training introduces a structured workflow for building,
evaluating, and interpreting species distribution models (SDMs) and
ecological niche models (ENMs), widely used in ecology, conservation
science, and biodiversity research.

Participants will work through practical examples and learn how to apply
best-practice modelling procedures to real ecological datasets.

The course covers:

   -

   Core concepts behind species distribution and ecological niche modelling
   -

   Preparing species occurrence and environmental predictor data
   -

   Model calibration, validation, and interpretation
   -

   Avoiding common modelling pitfalls and biases
   -

   Reproducible modelling workflows in R

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available
afterwards. Participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 23?24 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 5 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?275

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals who want a practical
introduction to SDMs and ENMs using standardised workflows in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Help needed: unmarkedMultFrame dimension mismatch in yearlySiteCovs
messages.subject_clean: Help needed: unmarkedMultFrame dimension mismatch in yearlySiteCovs
messages.body_plain:
Dear R-sig-ecology community,

I am currently using the unmarked package to create a unmarkedMultFrame for
analyzing wildlife data. I am facing an issue where the yearlySiteCovs
object, which I am passing as one of the arguments, contains elements that
appear as "arrays" rather than matrices, despite my efforts to convert them.

Here's the context:
yearlySiteCovs contains three periods (period1, period2, period3), each
having dimensions 368x18.
However, when I check the class of each element in yearlySiteCovs, I see
that they are reported as "arrays" instead of matrices.
Even after using functions like as.matrix() and drop(), the elements are
still identified as arrays, which is causing issues when I try to run the
unmarkedMultFrame model.

Example script:
# My yearlySiteCovs is a list containing three periods
yearlySiteCovs <- list(
  period1 = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 368, ncol = 18),
  period2 = matrix(c(2, 3, 4, 5), nrow = 368, ncol = 18),
  period3 = matrix(c(3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 368, ncol = 18)
)
# Attempt to convert to matrix
yearlySiteCovs <- lapply(yearlySiteCovs, function(x) {
  if (is.array(x)) {
    x <- as.vector(x)  # Flatten the array
    x <- matrix(x, nrow = 368, ncol = 18, byrow = TRUE)
  }
  return(x)
})

sapply(yearlySiteCovs, function(x) c(class(x), dim(x)))
    period1  period2  period3
[1,] "matrix" "matrix" "matrix"
*[2,] "array"  "array"  "array"*
[3,] "368"    "368"    "368"
[4,] "18"     "18"     "18"

Despite these efforts, the issue persists, and I receive the error:

"*At least one element of yearlySiteCovs has an incorrect number of
dimensions.*"

What I've tried:
I have used as.matrix(), drop(), and other standard methods to flatten and
convert the arrays into matrices.
I verified the dimensions of all the objects in yearlySiteCovs, and they
all appear to have the correct dimensions (368 rows and 18 columns).
I confirmed the format of y_matrix_processed, siteCovs, and obs_covs, all
of which are correctly formatted.
Could anyone help me understand why the "array" issue persists, and how I
can resolve it to ensure that yearlySiteCovs works correctly with
unmarkedMultFrame?

Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you in advance!

-- 

*Mohamed EL Mekkaoui*
*Doctorant - Chercheur* *Laboratoire de Biodiversit?, ?cologie et G?nome (*
BGCG*)* Facult? des Sciences, Universit? Mohammed V de Rabat

*LinkedIn :* <http://goog_85098632>linkedin.com/in/mohamed-el-mekkaoui

*Email :* mohamedelmekkaoui98 at gmail.com

*T?l :* +212 7 08 00 50 71

--

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] online course: Network Analysis in R,
messages.subject_clean: online course: Network Analysis in R,
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are excited to announce our upcoming online course: Network Analysis in R, taking place from 4?7 May.
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-analysis-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-analysis-in-r/ ) 
 
This hands-on course will teach you how to analyse, model, and visualize network data in R, with practical exercises and live coding sessions every day. You will learn to explore network structures, calculate network statistics, detect clusters, perform statistical modeling, and create clear, informative visualizations. While the focus is on life sciences, the skills are fully transferable to other fields.
 
You may also be interested in our companion course Network Analysis in Systems Biology with R/Bioconductor, which links networks with multi-omics data and biological function.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-in-systems-biology/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/network-in-systems-biology/ ) 
 
 
 
Best regards, Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Network Analysis for Ecologists - Live online and on demand options
messages.subject_clean: Network Analysis for Ecologists - Live online and on demand options
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse ecological networks with our course *Network Analysis
for Ecologists (NWAE02 / NWAEPR)*, available in both live online and
recorded formats.

Live course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwae02/
Recorded course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwaepr/

This applied R training introduces network analysis methods used to study
ecological interactions such as food webs, species co-occurrence, movement
networks, host?parasite systems, and social or behavioural networks.
Participants learn practical workflows for constructing, analysing, and
interpreting ecological networks using real datasets.

The course covers:

   -

   Concepts and terminology in ecological network analysis
   -

   Building and visualising ecological networks
   -

   Network metrics and interpretation
   -

   Community detection and modularity
   -

   Statistical approaches to testing ecological network hypotheses
   -

   Reproducible workflows in R

*Two flexible learning options*

*Live Online Course*

*Course details*
Dates: 3-4 November 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 3 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?250

   -

   Real-time interaction with the instructor
   -

   Opportunity to ask questions and discuss your own data
   -

   Structured schedule to stay on track
   -

   Networking with other participants

*Recorded Course*

*Course details*
Dates: Learn at your own pace
Duration: Approximately 12 hours
Format: Recorded with email/zoom support for questions
Fee: ?250

   -

   Learn at your own pace and revisit material anytime
   -

   Ideal for different time zones or busy schedules
   -

   Pause, replay, and review complex topics
   -

   Permanent access to lectures and materials

Both options include full course materials, datasets, and post-course
support.

*Who is this course for?*
Postgraduate students, researchers, and professionals working with
ecological interactions, biodiversity data, food webs, social networks,
movement connectivity, or species co-occurrence datasets.

Full details and registration:
Live course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwae02/
Recorded course:
https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwaepr/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER & MixSIAR (SIMM12)
messages.subject_clean: Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER & MixSIAR (SIMM12)
messages.body_plain:
Join our live online course *Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER &
MixSIAR (SIMM12)* and gain practical skills in applying stable isotope
mixing models in R.

https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/

Instructors - Prof. Andrew Parnell and Prof. Andrew Jackson, authors of the
SIBER package - who better to learn from than the package authors
themselves!

This year we have a guest speaker, Dr Bernd Freymann, Director of ?
https://isolife.nl/ ? He will talk about isolife and their work. Isolife
develop, produce and sell depleted, partially, and uniformly 13C-labelled
(0.1 to >97 atom %13C) compounds derived from food plants, trees, herbs,
algae, and fungi. These intrinsically labelled compounds are used as Stable
Isotope Tracers and Internal Standards in research and diagnostics in the
Life Sciences. Isolife delivers their products to private companies and
public research organisations worldwide.

*More about the course* - This hands-on training provides a structured
introduction to the theory and implementation of mixing models used to
quantify source contributions in ecological and biogeochemical studies.
Participants will work through practical examples using widely adopted R
packages, learning how to fit, compare, and interpret models appropriately.

The course covers:

   -

   Foundations of stable isotope ecology and mixing model theory
   -

   Preparing and structuring isotope datasets
   -

   Implementing models in SIBER, SIAR, and MixSIAR
   -

   Understanding assumptions, uncertainty, and diagnostics
   -

   Interpreting outputs for ecological research

Delivered live online, this course allows for real-time interaction with
the instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own
datasets. All participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 27-30 April 2026
Duration: 4 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online (with recorded access afterward)
Fee: ?480

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and
professionals working in ecology, conservation, fisheries science, or
environmental research who want to apply isotope mixing models confidently
and rigorously.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Learn how to build interactive data applications using R Shiny in our live online course Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01).
messages.subject_clean: Learn how to build interactive data applications using R Shiny in our live online course Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01).
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to build interactive data applications using *R Shiny* in our
live online course *Interactive Data Applications with Shiny (SHID01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/

This practical training teaches you how to develop, deploy, and customise
interactive web applications directly from R. Whether you?re creating
dashboards for collaborators, visualising complex datasets, or building
tools for reproducible reporting, this course provides structured, hands-on
instruction from foundational concepts to advanced Shiny workflows.

The course covers:

   -

   Introduction to R Shiny fundamentals
   -

   Designing reactive user interfaces
   -

   Building interactive visualisations and data tools
   -

   Modular app design and organisation
   -

   Deploying applications online and version control integration
   -

   Best practices for reproducibility and performance

Delivered live online, the course offers real-time interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own use
cases. All participants receive course materials, example code, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 28-29 April 2026
Duration: 2 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?300

This course is ideal for researchers, data scientists, analysts, and
professionals who want practical skills in building interactive data
applications that enhance data exploration, communication, and
reproducibility.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/interactive-data-applications-with-shiny-shid01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian Modelling Using R & INLA - Dr Virgilio Gómez-Rubio
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Modelling Using R & INLA - Dr Virgilio Gómez-Rubio
messages.body_plain:
Advance your statistical modelling skills with our live online course *Bayesian
Modelling Using R & INLA (BMIN04)*.

https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin04/

Instructor - Dr Virgilio G?mez-Rubio, a lead expert in the application of
INLA and the author of 'Bayesian Inference with INLA'.

This applied training provides a practical introduction to Bayesian
inference using the Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA)
framework in R. Participants will learn both conceptual foundations and
hands-on implementation of Bayesian models for complex data structures,
with workflows suitable for spatial, temporal, and hierarchical analyses.

The course covers:

   -

   Bayesian fundamentals and interpretation
   -

   Specification and fitting of Bayesian models in R using INLA
   -

   Model selection, comparison, and diagnostics
   -

   Handling spatial, temporal, and spatio-temporal data
   -

   Hierarchical and mixed-effects modelling in a Bayesian framework
   -

   Practical strategies for reproducible Bayesian workflows

Delivered live online, this course offers direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions about your own data, and
structured guidance through practical examples. All participants receive
course materials, example code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 4-8 May 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 7 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?500

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, analysts,
and professionals interested in applying Bayesian methods to real data
using R and INLA.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-modelling-using-r-inla-bmin04/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 – livestream seminar
messages.subject_clean: Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 – livestream seminar
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Instats is offering a new 1-day seminar Deep Learning for Complex Datasets with AI 3.0 livestreaming March 25, led by Nikolay Oskolkov (Group Leader (PI) at LIOS). This seminar explores deep learning techniques for analyzing complex datasets, offering powerful methods to uncover intricate patterns and hidden structures within your ecological and environmental data. Participants will learn to apply advanced neural network architectures, including convolutional and recurrent networks, to model time-series, spatial, and high-dimensional data relevant to vegetation, soil, and hydrology studies. The practical skills gained using R, Python, TensorFlow, and AI tools like ChatGPT will directly enhance your ability to develop sophisticated analytical workflows and extract novel insights from your research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/deep-learning-for-complex-datasets-with-4) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms
messages.body_plain:
Develop advanced Bayesian modelling skills in our live online course *Bayesian
Statistical Modelling with Stan and brms (BMSB01)*.

This hands-on workshop provides a comprehensive introduction to Bayesian
data analysis using *Stan* via the *brms*package in R. Participants learn
both the theoretical foundations of Bayesian inference and practical
workflows for fitting and interpreting Bayesian models with real datasets.

The course covers the full Bayesian modelling workflow, including how to
define priors, fit models, evaluate convergence, and interpret posterior
distributions.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Foundations of Bayesian reasoning and statistical inference
   -

   Fitting Bayesian regression models in R using the *brms* interface to
   Stan
   -

   Understanding likelihoods, priors, and posterior distributions
   -

   Model comparison and Bayesian diagnostics
   -

   Interpreting MCMC output (trace plots, R-hat, effective sample size)
   -

   Extending models to generalized linear and hierarchical models

Through practical coding exercises, participants gain experience
implementing Bayesian models and interpreting results in a modern
statistical workflow.

Delivered live online, the course allows direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of your own
modelling challenges. All participants receive course materials, example
code, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 5?7 May 2026
Duration: 3 days (approximately 6 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?400

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and
analysts who want to apply Bayesian methods to real datasets using R and
Stan.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-statistical-modelling-with-stan-and-brms-bmsb01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Movement Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data)
messages.subject_clean: Movement Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data)
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse animal tracking data in our live online course *Movement
Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data) (MOVE09)*.

https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/

Modern ecological research increasingly relies on movement datasets
collected from technologies such as GPS tags, biologgers, satellite
telemetry, and other tracking devices. This course provides a structured
introduction to the *movement ecology framework* and practical methods for
analysing movement data using R.

Participants will learn how to explore movement trajectories, quantify
spatial behaviour, and investigate how animals interact with their
environment.

*The course covers:*

   -

   Core concepts in movement ecology and tracking technologies
   -

   Preparing and exploring movement datasets
   -

   Home-range estimation and spatial movement metrics
   -

   Interaction metrics and behavioural interpretation
   -

   Resource selection and step-selection modelling
   -

   Reproducible workflows for analysing movement data in R

Through practical exercises and real examples, participants will gain
hands-on experience analysing movement trajectories and interpreting
ecological patterns from tracking data.

Delivered live online, the course allows direct interaction with the
instructor, opportunities to ask questions, and discussion of real research
applications. All participants receive course materials, example code, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 11?15 May 2026
Duration: 5 days (approximately 8 hours per day)
Format: Live online
Fee: ?480

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and environmental professionals working with animal tracking
or spatial movement data.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (Online course) Handling Missing Data in R -22–24 April
messages.subject_clean: (Online course) Handling Missing Data in R -22–24 April
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
In our upcoming online course ?Handling Missing Data in R? (22?24 April ), we will explore how to diagnose missing data patterns and select appropriate analytical strategies. Participants will learn how to distinguish between different missingness mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), visualise and assess missing data structures, and implement both traditional and modern imputation methods in R, including multiple imputation and iterative algorithms.
 
 
Through practical examples and hands-on exercises, the course will provide a clear framework to help researchers choose and justify the most appropriate approach for their specific datasets and research goals.
 
More information and registration details are available here: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/missing-data-with-r/ ) 
Best regards,
Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Evidence Synthesis and Meta-analysis in R | Online Course, 11–14 May
messages.subject_clean: Evidence Synthesis and Meta-analysis in R | Online Course, 11–14 May
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
You can still join our 4-day online course ?Meta-analysis in R? (11?14 May) to learn how to conduct rigorous and reproducible evidence synthesis in ecology and evolution.
 
In this hands-on course, participants will learn how to design and perform systematic reviews and meta-analyses, from question formulation and literature searching to effect size calculation, meta-analytic modelling, and interpretation of results. The course also covers multilevel meta-analysis, meta-regression, heterogeneity, publication bias, and data visualization, using R and widely used packages such as metafor and orchaRd.
 
The program combines lectures and practical exercises with real datasets, providing participants with the skills needed to apply evidence synthesis methods to their own research projects.
 
If you are interested in learning how to integrate findings across studies and perform robust meta-analyses in R, this course will provide the practical tools to get started: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/metain-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/metain-r/ ) 
 
Best regards,
 
Carlo
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Spatial Statistics in R for Ecological Data
messages.subject_clean: Spatial Statistics in R for Ecological Data
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Discover how to enhance your ecological research by mastering spatial data analysis techniques. Spatial Statistics in R led by Nick Bearman (University College London and Geospatial Training Solutions) is now available as an on-demand streaming seminar. This workshop introduces spatial statistics using R, a vital skill for understanding patterns and relationships in geographically referenced data. Ecologists and environmental scientists can apply these techniques to analyze spatial dependencies in vegetation, soil, or hydrological data, enriching their research outcomes with nuanced spatial insights. Participants will learn to use R and GeoDa for exploratory spatial data analysis, calculate Moran's I and LISA, and perform spatial regressions, enabling more robust analysis of their own ecological datasets.

Access this seminar (https://instats.org/seminar/spatial-statistics-in-r-1) whenever convenient and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
